verses.text: Sennacherib seizes Judah's fortified cities and sends Rabshakeh with a great army to threaten Jerusalem. The crisis comes to the gates. God's people face an enemy far beyond their strength.
Sennacherib seizes Judah's fortified cities and sends Rabshakeh with a great army to threaten Jerusalem. The crisis comes to the gates. God's people face an enemy far beyond their strength.
Sennacherib seizes Judah's fortified cities and sends Rabshakeh with a great army to threaten Jerusalem. The crisis comes to the gates. God's people face an enemy far beyond their strength.

Rabshakeh sneers, 'on whom dost thou trust?' mocking reliance on Egypt, 'a broken reed,' and on the LORD whose altars Hezekiah had reformed. He twists faith into folly. The enemy attacks the very confidence that should save.

Rabshakeh sneers, 'on whom dost thou trust?' mocking reliance on Egypt, 'a broken reed,' and on the LORD whose altars Hezekiah had reformed. He twists faith into folly. The enemy attacks the very confidence that should save.

He boasts that he comes up against the land by the LORD's own bidding, claiming God's sanction for his attack. He cloaks pride in piety. The wicked will even claim God's authority to shake the faithful.

He boasts that he comes up against the land by the LORD's own bidding, claiming God's sanction for his attack. He cloaks pride in piety. The wicked will even claim God's authority to shake the faithful.

He boasts that he comes up against the land by the LORD's own bidding, claiming God's sanction for his attack. He cloaks pride in piety. The wicked will even claim God's authority to shake the faithful.

Hezekiah's men ask him to speak in Aramaic, not Hebrew, but he refuses, addressing the people on the wall to frighten them. He aims past the leaders to the crowd. The enemy seeks to spread fear among the weak.

Hezekiah's men ask him to speak in Aramaic, not Hebrew, but he refuses, addressing the people on the wall to frighten them. He aims past the leaders to the crowd. The enemy seeks to spread fear among the weak.
He shouts, 'let not Hezekiah deceive you,' urging them not to trust the LORD to deliver. He targets their faith directly. The great aim of the tempter is to make God's people doubt their God.
He shouts, 'let not Hezekiah deceive you,' urging them not to trust the LORD to deliver. He targets their faith directly. The great aim of the tempter is to make God's people doubt their God.
He shouts, 'let not Hezekiah deceive you,' urging them not to trust the LORD to deliver. He targets their faith directly. The great aim of the tempter is to make God's people doubt their God.

He offers terms of surrender and asks, what god of any nation has delivered its land? shall the LORD save Jerusalem? He lumps God with idols. The proud blasphemer cannot tell the living God from the gods of wood.

The people held their peace at the king's command, and his officers came to Hezekiah with torn clothes. Wise silence met the taunt. They brought the blasphemy to the king rather than answering the scoffer.
